title: Can quantum-mechanical description of physical reality be considered complete?
text:
Abstract
In a complete theory there is an element corresponding to each element of reality. A sufficient condition for the reality of a physical quantity is the possibility of predicting it with certainty, without disturbing the system. In quantum mechanics in the case of two physical quantities described by non-commuting operators, the knowledge of one precludes the knowledge of the other. Then either (1) the description of reality given by the wave function in quantum mechanics is not complete or (2) these two quantities cannot have simultaneous reality. Consideration of the problem of making predictions concerning a system on the basis of measurements made on another system that had previously interacted with it leads to the result that if (1) is false then (2) is also false. One is thus led to conclude that the description of reality as given by a wave function is not complete.

title: Can quantum-mechanical description of physical reality be considered complete?

title: Ian McPherson
text:
E-mail
  
  
    
     LinkedIn
  
  
      ORCID
  

  
  


Welcome to my website! My name is Ian, and I am currently pursuing a PhD in Applied Mathematics and Statistics under the joint supervision of Mauro Maggioni and Mateo Díaz at Johns Hopkins University.



I am interested in utilizing classical mathematical structures to give provable guarantees in non-classical spaces. At the core I am inspired by the interplay of optimization, geometry, and statistics through the frameworks of Applied Harmonic Analysis, Riemannian Geometry, Optimal Transport, and Variational Analysis. I am most interested in translating these ideas into applications in data science, machine learning, and large-scale optimization.




(On-Going) Wasserstein Barycenters on Unknown Submanifolds of Wasserstein Space, with Mauro Maggioni  Motivating Question: Given only a local sampler of a non-convex submanifold of Wasserstein space, is there an algorithm to compute the Fréchet mean on the submanifold for a fixed number of points up to arbitrary accuracy? 
(On-Going) Nonsmooth Riemannian Optimization with Inexact Information, with Mateo Díaz and Benjamin Grimmer  Motivating Question: Can nonsmooth convex Riemannian optimization admit nonasymptotic convergence rates using only subgradients, first-order retractions, and vector transports? 
(Submitted 12/31) Neural Dynamic Portfolio Control with Provable Learning Guarantees, with Yizhe Huang, Rui Gao, Shuang Li, Luhao Zhang  Motivating Question: Are there neural approaches to portfolio control that incorporate historical returns with end-to-end (algorithmic, generalization, and regularization) global guarantees?
